card.intuition: Encode a subset of n items as an n-bit integer (bitmask). dp[mask][v] = best cost to have visited exactly the nodes in mask, currently at node v. Transition: try adding any unvisited node next. With n ≤ 20, 2ⁿ states × n last nodes is tractable.
card.analogy: Tracking which cities you've visited on a road trip using a checklist. Each unique checklist state (bitmask) combined with your current location fully captures where you are in the trip — regardless of the order you visited them.
when_to_use: Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) for small n (n ≤ 20)
Assigning n tasks to n workers optimally
Any problem where you must track which subset of items has been used
gotchas: Bitmask for n items uses bits 0..n-1: bit i is set iff item i is included
Iterate over submasks of a mask: for sub = mask; sub > 0; sub = (sub-1) & mask
n ≤ 20 is a soft limit — 2²⁰ ≈ 1M states; beyond 20 typically exceeds memory

exercise.prompt: Solve TSP with bitmask DP: find the minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle.
hints: new_mask sets bit v in mask: mask | (1 << v) — this marks city v as now visited
